retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Endocrine
retrieved_docs.content: Poorly managed maternal type 2 diabetes mellitus is the most common cause of large for gestational age (LGA) newborns, as high insulin levels stimulate fetal growth and contribute to increased birth weight. However, infants born to mothers with uncontrolled type 1 diabetes mellitus are typically small for gestational age due to a lack of insulin secretion and have a high mortality rate if they do not make it to full term. If they do not survive, 75% of these deaths are due to respiratory distress syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, clinicians should be aware of maturity-onset diabetes of the young, an autosomal dominant genetic condition that is often misdiagnosed as GDM. An estimated 5% of patients diagnosed with GDM have maturity-onset diabetes of the young. In patients with findings during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) of a fasting glucose of more than or equal to 5.5 mmol/L and a glucose increment from the fasting to 2-hour OGTT result of less than 4.6 mmol/L with a positive family history of diabetes, maturity-onset diabetes of the young should be suspected. Fasting glucose more than or equal to 100 mg/dL in patients with a body mass index (BMI) less than 25 kg/m 2 can also be used to help differentiate these conditions. [3] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young ," for more information on this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress due to decreased surfactant levels in preterm infants and hypoglycemia resulting from fetal hyperinsulinemia have been reported in infants of diabetic mothers. Macrosomic infants are at increased risk of shoulder dystocia during delivery. Additionally, these pregnancies are associated with a higher incidence of perinatal mortality, including an increased risk of stillbirth and delivery-related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, the evolution of diagnostic criteria for GDM has reflected ongoing scientific inquiry and shifting standards in maternal-fetal medicine. From the adaptation of glucose thresholds by international bodies like the World Health Organization (WHO) to the endorsement of comprehensive criteria by the International Association of the Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups (IADPSG) in 2013, GDM management approaches continue to evolve. These advancements highlight not only the complexity of diagnosing GDM but also underscore the critical need for evidence-based guidelines that mitigate risks for both mothers and infants. As diagnostic strategies continue to refine, ongoing research aims to optimize maternal and perinatal outcomes while addressing the diverse healthcare needs of pregnant women globally. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kernicterus -- History and Physical -- Postnatal History
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital infections in infants can present with jaundice due to decreased glucuronidation or increased hemolysis. A baby born to a mother with diabetes is usually large for gestational age, which indicates a higher number of red blood cells with an increased rate of turnover leading to jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Deliveries -- Technique or Treatment -- Immediate Postpartum Neonatal Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: After the initial assessment is complete and the infant and mother are determined to be stable, breastfeeding can be encouraged, which will help the infant maintain its blood glucose levels. Infants at risk for hypoglycemia should have their blood sugar level checked within the first hour of life, and they need to be monitored closely for evidence of hypoglycemia for the first few days. Infants at high risk for hypoglycemia include those born to mothers with diabetes, gestational or pregestational, and large or small infants of gestational age. [34] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Transient neonatal diabetes mellitus usually occurs in small for gestational age infants. This condition is self-limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational age <38 weeks Jaundice at <24 hours of life Predischarge TSB or TcB close to the phototherapy threshold Evidence of hemolysis or rapidly rising TSB or TcB Phototherapy before hospital discharge Family history of a sibling or parent who required phototherapy or exchange transfusion Family history of RBC disorders, including G6PD deficiency Exclusive breastfeeding with suboptimal intake Scalp hematoma or significant bruising Down syndrome Large for gestational age infant of a mother with diabetes [34] Risk factors for hyperbilirubinemia neurotoxicity that lower the threshold for phototherapy and escalation of care include: Gestational age <38 weeks Albumen <3.0 g/dL Hemolytic conditions Sepsis Significant clinical instability from any cause [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Proper control of maternal glucose levels significantly reduces GDM risks, including macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycemia. The risk of preeclampsia decreases from 18% to 12% with treatment. [1] If GDM interventions, including dietary modifications or pharmacologic therapy, are implemented early enough, progression to type 2 diabetes in 10 years is reduced by 35% to 40%. Furthermore, even small reductions in BMI can result in a reduced risk for diabetes by 25%. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Maternal Blood -- Clinical Significance -- Gestational Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational diabetes has historically been defined as abnormal glucose tolerance with first onset or recognition during pregnancy. [10] Within the United States, the prevalence of gestational diabetes mellitus is roughly 6 percent. [11] Human placental lactogen secreted by the syncytiotrophoblasts of the placenta increases the overall insulin resistance. Since uncontrolled maternal blood glucose levels can result in several adverse outcomes for the mother and fetus, it is necessary to screen and treat gestational diabetes. The most common method is 2-step testing:

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infants of diabetic mothers (IDM) and large for gestational age infants experience fetal hyperinsulinism and increased peripheral glucose utilization, putting them at risk for hypoglycemia in the immediate postnatal period. [1] [4] [12] The placenta supplies the fetus with a direct source of glucose via facilitated diffusion, such that fetal glucose concentrations are proportional to maternal levels. Prolonged elevations in maternal glucose concentrations result in fetal hyperglycemia and pancreatic overstimulation to increase endogenous fetal insulin production. [12] These elevated levels of fetal insulin persist after birth and, in the absence of a continuous exogenous glucose source, result in increased glucose utilization and lower blood glucose concentrations. [12] IDM have a decreased ability to mobilize glycogen stores after birth and experience a relative adrenal insufficiency with decreased levels of catecholamines, further contributing to the risk of low blood glucose levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoglycemia (Nursing) -- Pearls and Other issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia is relatively common in neonates, particularly in mothers with uncontrolled diabetes. A 2017 study performed in Israel showed that 559 neonates out of 3595 neonates were observed to have a glucose level of less than 47 mg/dL. [11] Gestational glucose intolerance in the mother is usually attributed to the presence of human placental lactogen. Pregnant women with impaired glucose tolerance not responsive to diet or exercise can be started on insulin. Insulin does not cross the placenta although the fetus will be exposed to maternal hyperglycemia. Since the fetal pancreatic islet cells produce insulin starting at 10 weeks gestation, the fetal pancreas is capable of responding to hyperglycemia. Upon delivery, the newborn pancreas continues to secrete insulin although maternal hyperglycemia is withdrawn. Subsequently, the neonate’s glucose will decrease, resulting in an insulin-glucose imbalance and hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Etiology -- Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes increases the risk of stillbirth up to 5 times. [5] [6] [7] With optimal glycemic control, the risk of stillbirth may be reduced. [8] In those with type 1 diabetes, the stillbirth rate is 16.1 per 1000 births. Poor diabetic control determined by elevated glycosylated hemoglobin before pregnancy (odds ratio [OR] 1.03) and later in pregnancy (OR 1.06) was associated with stillbirth. In those with type 2 diabetes, the stillbirth rate is 22.9/1000 births. A higher body mass index (BMI) (OR 1.07) and elevated glycosylated hemoglobin before pregnancy (OR 1.02) were associated with stillbirth. Birth weight may be affected by diabetes and is also related to the risk of stillbirth. If birth weight is less than the tenth percentile, the risk for stillbirth is elevated 6 times in mothers with type 1 diabetes and 3 times in those with type 2 diabetes compared to fetuses weighing in the tenth to 90th percentile. With type 2 diabetes, the risk for stillbirth was 2-fold higher if the birth weight was over the 95th percentile. A significantly higher number of stillborns in women with type 2 diabetes are male sex. A third of the stillbirths associated with diabetes occur at term. The highest rate of stillbirth is in the 38th week for type 1 diabetes and in the 39th week for type 2 diabetes. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over several decades, GDM management approaches have continued to evolve due to alarmingly high perinatal mortality rates among women with diabetes during pregnancy. A significant leap came in 1964 with the publication of O’Sullivan and Mahan’s landmark study that defined specific diagnostic criteria for GDM in the United States. Their criteria, based on a 100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test, were designed to identify women at increased risk, aiming to correlate maternal hyperglycemia with adverse perinatal outcomes. Subsequent validation studies underscored the importance of these thresholds in predicting both immediate complications and long-term health risks for mothers and their children. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoglycemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia is relatively common in neonates, particularly in mothers with uncontrolled diabetes. A 2017 study performed in Israel showed that 559 neonates out of 3595 live births were observed to have a glucose level of less than 47 mg/dL. [16] Gestational glucose intolerance in the mother is usually attributed to the presence of human placental lactogen. Pregnant women with impaired glucose tolerance not responsive to diet or exercise should be started on insulin. Insulin does not cross the placenta, although the fetus is exposed to maternal hyperglycemia. Since the fetal pancreatic islet cells produce insulin at 10 weeks gestation, the fetal pancreas can respond to hyperglycemia. The newborn pancreas continues to secrete insulin upon delivery, although maternal hyperglycemia is withdrawn. Subsequently, the neonate’s glucose decreases, resulting in an insulin-glucose imbalance and hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Certain differences have been observed between type 1 and type 2 diabetes in pregnant women. [23] Type 1 diabetes has been associated with younger mothers at the time of pregnancy. There is also a higher rate of reported preterm deliveries and large-for-gestational-age births. In contrast, type 2 diabetes is typically associated with older maternal age, a higher maternal body mass index (BMI), and a greater burden of comorbidities at the time of pregnancy. In addition, type 2 diabetes is also associated with an increased risk of neonatal death compared to type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1440
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all classified as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, a neonatal disorder. This grouping indicates that both forms of transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes fall under the broader category of neonatal disorders, highlighting their importance in newborn assessment, monitoring, and specialized management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base describes a hierarchical taxonomy of perinatal and neonatal disorders that integrates gestational timing, birth weight, fetal growth, and specific clinical conditions across multiple languages and coding systems. A gestational period of 29–30 weeks is classified as خديج (preterm) and is a subtype of liveborn infant; خديج is causally linked to متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome) and to respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), which is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (newborn disorder). اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is a member of the SMQ group اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة and is associated with عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك (unspecified perinatal infection). Transient neonatal diabetes (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 1 and 2), developmental delay, epilepsy, and other neonatal metabolic disorders are also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة. Disorders relating to short gestation and/or low birthweight are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 258
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge maps a network of perinatal, neonatal and obstetric conditions anchored by gestational age, birth‑weight and metabolic disease. A gestational period of 29–30 weeks defines the Arabic preterm‑birth concept خديج, which is classified under the broader live‑born category and subsumes the Arabic newborn‑disorder concept اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة. This newborn‑disorder node is linked to متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (respiratory distress syndrome, RDS) and to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus), illustrating that preterm birth, low birth weight and fetal growth retardation (short gestation; low birth weight; fetal growth retardation) are clinically associated with both RDS and diabetes‑related complications such as toe amputation بتر أصبع القدم and transient neonatal diabetes mellitus (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 1 & 2) as well as the composite disorder “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes.” Diabetes is further connected to metabolic syndrome متلازمة

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: and extreme prematurity with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). These entities share parentage with unspecified perinatal conditions, which are children of perinatal infections (عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك) and are also part of the SMQ for unspecified perinatal infections. Collectively, the network demonstrates how preterm gestation, fetal growth restriction, respiratory distress, cerebral injury, diabetes mellitus, varicose veins, ulceration, and related amputation procedures interrelate, providing clinicians with a structured framework for identifying high‑risk infants, anticipating metabolic and vascular sequelae, and guiding preventive and therapeutic strategies to improve perinatal and long‑term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are both mapped to the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that they are classified as neonatal disorders. This mapping underscores that transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes represent related early‑life metabolic and neurological conditions, highlighting the need for integrated recognition and management of such disorders in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader category Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it is a subset of the clinical indications guiding obstetric management. Within this group of obstetric complications, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication, highlighting that gestational diabetes and related glucose intolerance are important conditions to consider when evaluating and treating patients during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.
